utils¶

utils.load_llm module¶

LLM Loading and Configuration Module.

Provides utilities for loading pre-trained LLMs and tokenizers with optional quantization, layer truncation, LoRA fine-tuning, and trainable parameter configuration.

Example

>>> loader = LLMLoader(cfg)
>>> llm, tokenizer = loader.load_llm_and_tokenizer()
>>> llm = loader.truncate_llm_layers(llm)
>>> llm = loader.prepare_lora(llm)
>>> llm = loader.define_trainable_params(llm)
>>> _ = loader.summarize_configuration(llm)
class utils.load_llm.LLMLoader(cfg: DictConfig)[source]¶

Bases: object

Loads and configures a pre-trained LLM for time-series reasoning.

Handles tokenizer and model loading with optional 4-bit or 8-bit quantization, layer truncation, LoRA adapter injection, and trainable parameter selection. Configuration summaries are saved to disk as JSON.

cfg¶

Hydra configuration object. Must contain llm subconfig sub-configs with the fields name, dir, quantization, num_hidden_layers, output_attentions, output_hidden_states, lora_config, and training.save_results_dir_path. And model sub-configs with the field trainable_llm_params.

Type:

DictConfig

define_trainable_params(llm) → Module[source]¶

Sets trainable parameters based on name pattern matching.

Iterates over all LLM parameters and enables gradients only for those whose name matches any pattern in trainable_llm_params. Non-floating-point parameters are always frozen.

Parameters:

llm (nn.Module) – LLM whose parameters to configure.

Returns:

LLM with requires_grad set per trainable_llm_params.

Return type:

nn.Module

load_llm_and_tokenizer() → tuple[AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer][source]¶

Loads the tokenizer and LLM with optional quantization.

Loads the tokenizer from dir and the causal LLM with the configured quantization. If the tokenizer has no pad token, the EOS token is used as a fallback, or a new [PAD] token is added if EOS is also absent.

Returns:

A tuple of

(llm, tokenizer) ready for downstream use.

Return type:

tuple[AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer]

prepare_lora(llm) → Module[source]¶

Injects LoRA adapters into the LLM using the configured LoRA settings.

Wraps the LLM with PEFT’s get_peft_model using a peft.LoraConfig built from lora_config.

Parameters:

llm (nn.Module) – LLM to inject LoRA adapters into.

Returns:

PEFT-wrapped LLM with LoRA adapters applied.

Return type:

nn.Module

summarize_configuration(llm) → dict[str, str | int | float][source]¶

Saves a JSON summary of the LLM configuration and parameter counts.

Computes total, trainable, and non-trainable parameter counts, and saves a summary alongside the LoRA and training configuration to save_results_dir_path/llm_summary.json.

Parameters:

llm (nn.Module) – Configured LLM to summarize.

Returns:

Summary dictionary containing model name, quantization,

parameter counts, trainable percentage, device, dtype, and memory footprint.

Return type:

dict[str, str | int | float]

truncate_llm_layers(llm) → Module[source]¶

Truncates the LLM to the configured number of hidden layers.

Supports GPT-2 style (llm.h), GPT-2 with transformer wrapper (llm.transformer.h), and LLaMA/Mistral style (llm.model.layers) architectures. If truncation fails, a warning is logged and all layers are kept.

Parameters:

llm (nn.Module) – Loaded causal LLM to truncate.

Returns:

LLM with at most num_hidden_layers transformer layers.

Return type:

nn.Module

utils.model_summarizer module¶

Utilities for summarizing and aggregating PyTorch model parameter statistics.

This module provides ModelSummarizer, which inspects a Module, counts total and trainable parameters per top-level child module, serializes the results to JSON, and can later average those JSON files across multiple dataset runs to produce a single consolidated report.

Typical usage inside a training loop:

from src.utils.model_summarizer import ModelSummarizer

summarizer = ModelSummarizer(cfg, save_dir)

# After instantiating the model for each dataset:
summary = summarizer.summarize_model_parameters(model, dataset_name)

# After all datasets have been processed:
avg = summarizer.average_model_summaries()

Example

>>> summarizer = ModelSummarizer(cfg, Path("results/"))
>>>
>>> model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(64, 128), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(128, 10))
>>> summary = summarizer.summarize_model_parameters(model, "ArticularyWordRecognition")
>>> summary["trainable_parameters"]
9610
>>>
>>> avg = summarizer.average_model_summaries()
>>> avg["num_files_averaged"]
1
class utils.model_summarizer.ModelSummarizer(cfg: DictConfig, save_results_dir_path: Path)[source]¶

Bases: object

Computes, saves, and aggregates parameter summaries for PyTorch models.

For each model inspected via summarize_model_parameters() a JSON file is written to save_results_dir_path. After all runs are complete, average_model_summaries() reads every previously written file and produces a single averaged report — useful when the same architecture is trained on multiple datasets that may alter the number of classes or input channels.

cfg¶

Hydra/OmegaConf configuration object. The field cfg.model.name is embedded in every summary JSON.

Type:

DictConfig

save_results_dir_path¶

Directory in which JSON summaries are written. The directory must already exist; this class does not create it.

Type:

Path

Example

>>> from pathlib import Path
>>> from omegaconf import OmegaConf
>>> import torch.nn as nn
>>> cfg = OmegaConf.create({"model": {"name": "my_model"}})
>>> summarizer = ModelSummarizer(cfg, Path("results/"))
>>> model = nn.Linear(32, 10)
>>> summary = summarizer.summarize_model_parameters(model, "dataset_A")
>>> summary["total_parameters"]
330
average_model_summaries() → dict[str, object][source]¶

Averages all per-dataset parameter summaries into one report.

Globs every file matching model_parameters_*.json in save_results_dir_path, averages the top-level numeric fields and each module’s breakdown fields across all files, and writes the result to model_parameters_averaged.json in the same directory.

Modules that do not appear in every file (e.g. because the model architecture differs slightly across datasets) are still included; the "present_in_n_files" field records how many files contributed to that module’s average.

The returned (and saved) dictionary has the following structure:

{
    "model_name": str,
    "num_files_averaged": int,
    "total_parameters": float,
    "trainable_parameters": float,
    "non_trainable_parameters": float,
    "trainable_percent": float,
    "breakdown": {
        "<child_name>": {
            "total": float,
            "trainable": float,
            "trainable_percent": float,
            "percent_of_total_trainable": float,
            "present_in_n_files": int
        },
        ...
    }
}
Returns:

The averaged summary dictionary as described above.

Return type:

dict[str, Any]

Raises:

IndexError – If no model_parameters_*.json files are found in save_results_dir_path (summaries would be empty).

summarize_model_parameters(model: Module, data_name: str | None) → dict[str, object][source]¶

Counts parameters per top-level child module and writes a JSON summary.

Iterates over every direct child returned by named_children(), counts total and trainable parameters for each, and also computes overall model-level totals. The result is serialized to <save_results_dir_path>/model_parameters_<data_name>.json.

The returned (and saved) dictionary has the following structure:

{
    "model_name": str,
    "total_parameters": int,
    "trainable_parameters": int,
    "non_trainable_parameters": int,
    "trainable_percent": float,
    "breakdown": {
        "<child_name>": {
            "total": int,
            "trainable": int,
            "trainable_percent": float,
            "percent_of_total_trainable": float
        },
        ...
    }
}
Parameters:

  • model (nn.Module) – The PyTorch model to inspect. All parameters reachable via parameters() are counted, including those in nested sub-modules.

  • data_name (str | None) – Identifier appended to the output filename (e.g. a dataset name). If None, the file is named model_parameters_None.json.

Returns:

The summary dictionary as described above.

Return type:

dict[str, Any]

utils.seed module¶

Centralised seeding utilities for fully reproducible PyTorch experiments.

This module exposes SeedManager, a stateful helper that applies a single seed to every randomness source used in a typical training pipeline (Python, NumPy, PyTorch CPU/GPU, cuDNN) and provides the generator and worker-init function required by DataLoader.

Example

>>> from src.utils.seed import SeedManager
>>> sm = SeedManager(seed=42)
>>> loader = DataLoader(
...     dataset,
...     generator=sm.get_generator(),
...     worker_init_fn=sm.seed_worker,
... )
>>> checkpoint = {"model": model.state_dict(), "rng": sm.save_rng_state()}
>>> torch.save(checkpoint, "ckpt.pt")
>>> # On resume:
>>> sm.load_rng_state(torch.load("ckpt.pt")["rng"])
class utils.seed.SeedManager(seed: int = 2025)[source]¶

Bases: object

Stateful manager that centralises all seeding concerns for an experiment.

On construction the global seed is applied immediately and a dedicated torch.Generator is created. The same instance can then supply the generator and worker_init_fn directly to DataLoader, and can save / restore the full RNG state for mid-training checkpointing.

seed¶

Seed applied to all RNG sources. Defaults to 2025.

Type:

int

create_generator() → Generator[source]¶

Creates and seeds the internal torch.Generator.

Called once during __init__; the result is stored as self.generator and exposed via get_generator().

Returns:

A CPU generator seeded with self.seed.

Return type:

torch.Generator

get_generator() → Generator[source]¶

Returns the seeded torch.Generator for DataLoader use.

Using a dedicated generator isolates DataLoader shuffle / sampling randomness from the global PyTorch RNG, so that changing the number of data-loading steps does not affect model weight initialisation or dropout.

Returns:

The CPU generator created during __init__, seeded with self.seed.

Return type:

torch.Generator

Example

>>> sm = SeedManager(42)
>>> loader = DataLoader(dataset, generator=sm.get_generator())
load_rng_state(state: dict) → None[source]¶

Restores RNG states from a checkpoint produced by save_rng_state().

After this call, all subsequent random operations across Python, NumPy, PyTorch CPU, and all CUDA devices will proceed as if the training run had never been interrupted.

Parameters:

state (dict) – Dictionary produced by save_rng_state(), containing the keys "python", "numpy", "torch", and "cuda".

Example

>>> ckpt = torch.load("ckpt.pt")
>>> sm = SeedManager(42)
>>> sm.load_rng_state(ckpt["rng"])
save_rng_state() → dict[source]¶

Captures the current RNG state of every randomness source.

Returns a snapshot that can be saved alongside a model checkpoint and later restored with load_rng_state() to resume training with bit-exact reproducibility.

Returns:

A dictionary with the following keys:

  • "python" – state object from random.getstate().

  • "numpy" – state tuple from numpy.random.get_state().

  • "torch" – CPU RNG state tensor from torch.get_rng_state().

  • "cuda" – list of per-device GPU RNG state tensors from torch.cuda.get_rng_state_all() (empty list if no CUDA devices are available).

Return type:

dict

Example

>>> sm = SeedManager(42)
>>> torch.save({"rng": sm.save_rng_state()}, "ckpt.pt")
seed_worker(worker_id: int) → None[source]¶

Seeds a DataLoader worker process for reproducible data loading.

Derives a unique, deterministic seed for each worker from PyTorch’s initial_seed() (controlled by the main-process seed) offset by worker_id. Pass this bound method as worker_init_fn to DataLoader.

Parameters:

worker_id (int) – Zero-based index of the worker process, supplied automatically by DataLoader.

Example

>>> sm = SeedManager(42)
>>> loader = DataLoader(
...     dataset,
...     num_workers=4,
...     worker_init_fn=sm.seed_worker,
...     generator=sm.get_generator(),
... )
set_global_seed() → None[source]¶

Applies self.seed to every randomness source in the pipeline.

Covers the Python hash seed (PYTHONHASHSEED), the random module, NumPy, PyTorch CPU, all CUDA devices, and cuDNN (deterministic kernels, auto-tuning disabled). Called automatically during __init__ and can be called again after loading a checkpoint to re-apply the seed.

Note

torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True and torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False guarantee reproducibility at the cost of reduced GPU throughput. Disable these flags for inference-only workloads.
